sections.heading: Complications
sections.content:
Transformation to aggressive B-cell lymphoma (e.g., diffuse large B-cell lymphoma): Management: Biopsy recommended for suspected relapse, especially at intra-abdominal or splenic sites. Treatment per NCCN Guidelines for B-Cell Lymphomas.
B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%): Management: Part of staging and prognostic assessment; treated with systemic therapy.
Large mediastinal mass with potential for airway compromise or superior vena cava syndrome: Management: Urgent systemic therapy and/or radiation; consider steroids for rapid cytoreduction.
Secondary malignancies (solid tumors, leukemia, myelodysplastic syndrome): Risk is highest with extended-field RT and alkylating agents. Lung and breast cancers are most common [9,10]. Management: Monitoring and screening per guidelines.
Pulmonary toxicity (bleomycin): Pneumonitis, fibrosis. Risk factors: age >40, concurrent G-CSF, prior lung disease. Monitor with PFTs (DLCO) [11].
Cardiac toxicity (anthracyclines): Cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure. Risk increases with cumulative dose. Monitor with echocardiography [12].
Infertility (alkylating agents, escalated BEACOPP): Risk of premature ovarian failure and azoospermia. Counseling and fertility preservation recommended [13].
Neuropathy (vinca alkaloids, BV): Peripheral sensory neuropathy; dose modifications or discontinuation may be required.
Immune-mediated adverse events (nivolumab, pembrolizumab): Hypothyroidism, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis. Monitor with TFTs and clinical assessment [14].
Infusion-related reactions (rituximab): Premedicate with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine.
Brentuximab vedotin (BV): Peripheral neuropathy, neutropenia, infusion-related reactions. PJP prophylaxis recommended for all BV-containing regimens [15].
Infertility from pelvic surgery or radiation; fertility preservation should be discussed.
Secondary malignancies: Breast, lung, thyroid, gastrointestinal cancers. Risk increases linearly with dose [10].
Cardiovascular disease: Coronary artery disease, valvular heart disease, cardiomyopathy. Risk is linear with mean heart dose; even 5-15 Gy increases risk [16].
Hypothyroidism: Occurs in up to 40% of long-term survivors after neck/mediastinal RT. Lifetime screening required [17].
Pneumonitis: Risk related to mean lung dose, V20, V5. Limit MLD <13.5 Gy, V20 <20% [18].
Diabetes mellitus (pancreatic RT): Mean dose ≥21 Gy increases risk [19].
Chronic renal insufficiency (abdominal RT): Minimize V5, V10, V20 to kidneys [20].
Gonadal toxicity: Ovarian failure (mean dose <2 Gy recommended; <10 Gy acceptable); azoospermia (scatter doses as low as 0.2 Gy) [21,22].

cards.name: Hypothyroidism
cards.detail: Occurs in up to 40% of long-term survivors after neck/mediastinal RT. Lifetime screening required [17].
